subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Thomson's Model of an Atom: An atom consists of positively charged matter in which the negatively charged electrons are uniformly embedded like plums in a pudding. This model could not explain scattering of alpha-particles through thin foils and hence discarded.
text_html: Thomson's Model of an Atom: An atom consists of positively charged matter in which the negatively charged electrons are uniformly embedded like plums in a pudding. This model could not explain scattering of alpha-particles through thin foils and hence discarded.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Rutherford's Model of an Atom: Geiger and Marsden in their experiment on scattering of alpha-particles found that most of the alpha-particles passed un deviated through thin foils but some of them were scattered through very large angles. From the results of these experiments, Rutherford proposed the following model of an atom:- An atom consists of a small and massive central core in which the entire positive charge and almost the whole mass of the atom are concentrated. This core is called the nucleus. The nucleus occupies a very small space as compared to the size of the atom. The atom is surrounded by a suitable number of electros so that their total negative charge is equal to the total positive charge on the nucleus and the atom as a whole is electrically neutral. The electrons revolve around the nucleus in various orbits just as planets revolve around the sun. The centripetal force required for their revolution is provided by the electrostatic attraction between the electrons and the nucleus.
text_html:
Rutherford's Model of an Atom: Geiger and Marsden in their experiment on scattering of alpha-particles found that most of the alpha-particles passed un deviated through thin foils but some of them were scattered through very large angles.
From the results of these experiments, Rutherford proposed the following model of an atom:-

  1. An atom consists of a small and massive central core in which the entire positive charge and almost the whole mass of the atom are concentrated. This core is called the nucleus.
  2. The nucleus occupies a very small space as compared to the size of the atom.
  3. The atom is surrounded by a suitable number of electros so that their total negative charge is equal to the total positive charge on the nucleus and the atom as a whole is electrically neutral.
  4. The electrons revolve around the nucleus in various orbits just as planets revolve around the sun.
  5. The centripetal force required for their revolution is provided by the electrostatic attraction between the electrons and the nucleus.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Draw-back of Rutherford Model This model could not explain instability of the atom because according to classical electromagnetic theory the electron revolving around the nucleus must continuously radiate energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation and hence it should fall into the nucleus.
text_html: Draw-back of Rutherford Model This model could not explain instability of the atom because according to classical electromagnetic theory the electron revolving around the nucleus must continuously radiate energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation and hence it should fall into the nucleus.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Impact Parameter: It is defined as the perpendicular distance of the velocity of the alpha-particle from the centre of the nucleus, when it is far away from the atom. The shape of the trajectory of the scattered alpha-particle depends on the impact parameter b and the nature of the potential field. Rutherford deduced the following relationship between the impact parameter b and the scattering angle {tex}\theta{/tex} .
text_html:
Impact Parameter:

  1. It is defined as the perpendicular distance of the velocity of the alpha-particle from the centre of the nucleus, when it is far away from the atom.
  2. The shape of the trajectory of the scattered alpha-particle depends on the impact parameter b and the nature of the potential field.
  3. Rutherford deduced the following relationship between the impact parameter b and the scattering angle {tex}\theta{/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Geiger-Marsden Scattering Experiment The scattering experiment was conducted by Geiger and Marsden in 1911 under Rutherford's direction using a {tex}5.5 MeV{/tex} beam of alpha-particles from a {tex}^{214}_{83}Bi{/tex} radioactive source directed at a thin gold foil.
text_html: Geiger-Marsden Scattering Experiment The scattering experiment was conducted by Geiger and Marsden in 1911 under Rutherford's direction using a {tex}5.5 MeV{/tex} beam of alpha-particles from a {tex}^{214}_{83}Bi{/tex} radioactive source directed at a thin gold foil.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Apparatus and Setup The experiment apparatus was enclosed in a vacuum chamber. Alpha-particles were made into a narrow beam and directed at gold foil of thickness {tex}2.1 \times 10^{-7} m{/tex} . Scintillations on a zinc sulfide screen detected scattered particles.
text_html: Apparatus and Setup The experiment apparatus was enclosed in a vacuum chamber. Alpha-particles were made into a narrow beam and directed at gold foil of thickness {tex}2.1 \times 10^{-7} m{/tex}. Scintillations on a zinc sulfide screen detected scattered particles.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Scattering Observations - Most alpha-particles passed through without incident, implying few collisions - Approximately {tex}0.14\%{/tex} scattered more than {tex}1^{\circ}{/tex} , while 1 in 8000 deflected over {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} - Rutherford argued a dense nucleus caused the deflections.
text_html: Scattering Observations   - Most alpha-particles passed through without incident, implying few collisions - Approximately {tex}0.14\%{/tex} scattered more than {tex}1^{\circ}{/tex}, while 1 in 8000 deflected over {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} - Rutherford argued a dense nucleus caused the deflections.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Nuclear Model of the Atom - Positive charge and most mass in a dense nucleus - Electrons orbit the nucleus like planets around the Sun - Nucleus estimated between {tex}10^{-15}{/tex} to {tex}10^{-14} m{/tex} - Atom mostly empty space enabling alpha-particles to pass through.
text_html: Nuclear Model of the Atom   - Positive charge and most mass in a dense nucleus - Electrons orbit the nucleus like planets around the Sun - Nucleus estimated between {tex}10^{-15}{/tex} to {tex}10^{-14} m{/tex} - Atom mostly empty space enabling alpha-particles to pass through.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Trajectory of Alpha-Particles The trajectory relies on the impact parameter, the perpendicular distance from the particle's starting velocity to the nucleus. A small impact parameter results in large scattering, including rebounds for head-on collisions.
text_html: Trajectory of Alpha-Particles The trajectory relies on the impact parameter, the perpendicular distance from the particle's starting velocity to the nucleus. A small impact parameter results in large scattering, including rebounds for head-on collisions.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Force between Particles and Nucleus Alpha-particles experience a force due to the nucleus described by Coulomb's Law: {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(2e)(Ze)}{r^2}{/tex} , where {tex}r{/tex} is the distance between them.
text_html: Force between Particles and Nucleus Alpha-particles experience a force due to the nucleus described by Coulomb's Law: {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(2e)(Ze)}{r^2}{/tex}, where {tex}r{/tex} is the distance between them.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Rutherford Scattering Analysis Rutherford's model assumes single scattering events in thin gold. It computes alpha-particle trajectories using Newton's second law and Coulomb's law, assuming the stationary gold nucleus due to its mass.
text_html: Rutherford Scattering Analysis Rutherford's model assumes single scattering events in thin gold. It computes alpha-particle trajectories using Newton's second law and Coulomb's law, assuming the stationary gold nucleus due to its mass.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Electron Orbits and Energy Relationships - Orbit radius and velocity relationship: {tex}r=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 mv^2}{/tex} - Kinetic energy in orbit: {tex}K=\frac{1}{2}mv^2=\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} - Potential energy: {tex}U=-\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} - Total energy of electron: {tex}E=-\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}
text_html: Electron Orbits and Energy Relationships   - Orbit radius and velocity relationship: {tex}r=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 mv^2}{/tex} - Kinetic energy in orbit: {tex}K=\frac{1}{2}mv^2=\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} - Potential energy: {tex}U=-\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} - Total energy of electron: {tex}E=-\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Implications of Negative Total Energy The negative total energy of an electron signifies a bound state to the nucleus. A positive energy would mean the electron would escape the nuclear attraction.
text_html: Implications of Negative Total Energy The negative total energy of an electron signifies a bound state to the nucleus. A positive energy would mean the electron would escape the nuclear attraction.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Significance of Rutherford's Work Rutherford's findings led to the nuclear atomic model, disproving the previous plum pudding model, and established the concentrated mass and positive charge in the atomic center, essentially discovering the nucleus.
text_html: Significance of Rutherford's Work Rutherford's findings led to the nuclear atomic model, disproving the previous plum pudding model, and established the concentrated mass and positive charge in the atomic center, essentially discovering the nucleus.

subject: Physics
topic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
text: Experimental and Theoretical Correlation The experimental data showed strong correlation to Rutherford's nuclear model predictions. It supported the hypothesis of a central nucleus, thus advancing the understanding of atomic structure.
text_html: Experimental and Theoretical Correlation The experimental data showed strong correlation to Rutherford's nuclear model predictions. It supported the hypothesis of a central nucleus, thus advancing the understanding of atomic structure.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Bohr's Model for the Hydrogen Atom: Basic postulates:- Nuclear concept: An atom consists of a small massive centre called nucleus around which planetary electrons revolve. The centripetal force required for their rotation is provided by the electrostatic attraction between the electrons and the nucleus. Quantum condition: Of all the possible circular orbits allowed by the classical theory, the electrons are permitted to circulate only in such orbits in which the angular momentum of an electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} , h being Planck's constant.where n is called principal quantum number. Stationary orbits: While revolving in the permissible orbits, an electron does not radiate energy. These non-radiating orbits are called stationary orbits. Frequency condition: An atom can emit or absorb radiation in the form of discrete energy photons only when an electron jumps from a higher to a lower orbit or from a lower to a higher orbit. If E 1 and E 2 are the energies associated w frequency of the emitted/absorbed radiation is, hv = E 2 - E 1
text_html:
Bohr's Model for the Hydrogen Atom: Basic postulates:-

  • Nuclear concept: An atom consists of a small massive centre called nucleus around which planetary electrons revolve. The centripetal force required for their rotation is provided by the electrostatic attraction between the electrons and the nucleus.
  • Quantum condition: Of all the possible circular orbits allowed by the classical theory, the electrons are permitted to circulate only in such orbits in which the angular momentum of an electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}, h being Planck's constant.where n is called principal quantum number.
  • Stationary orbits: While revolving in the permissible orbits, an electron does not radiate energy. These non-radiating orbits are called stationary orbits.
  • Frequency condition: An atom can emit or absorb radiation in the form of discrete energy photons only when an electron jumps from a higher to a lower orbit or from a lower to a higher orbit. If E1 and E2 are the energies associated w frequency of the emitted/absorbed radiation is, hv = E2 - E1

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Bohr's Model for the Hydrogen Atom: Basic postulates:- Radius of the orbit of an electron in hydrogen atom is, {tex}r = \frac {e^z}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m v^2}{/tex} Kinetic energy K & electrostatic potential energy U of the electron in hydrogen atom:- {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} U = {tex}\frac {e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Total energy E of the electron in hydrogen atom:- E = K + U = {tex}-\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Speed of an electron in the nth orbit is, Where {tex}\alpha=\frac{2 \pi k e^2}{c h}{/tex} is fine structure constant.
text_html:
Bohr's Model for the Hydrogen Atom: Basic postulates:-

  • Radius of the orbit of an electron in hydrogen atom is, {tex}r = \frac {e^z}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m v^2}{/tex}
  • Kinetic energy K & electrostatic potential energy U of the electron in hydrogen atom:-
    {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}
    U = {tex}\frac {e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}
  • Total energy E of the electron in hydrogen atom:-
    E = K + U = {tex}-\frac{e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}
  • Speed of an electron in the nth orbit is,
    Where {tex}\alpha=\frac{2 \pi k e^2}{c h}{/tex} is fine structure constant.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Failure of Bohr's Model: This model is applicable only to hydrogen-like atoms and fails in case of higher atoms. It could not explain the fine structure of the spectral lines in the spectrum of hydrogen atom.
text_html:
Failure of Bohr's Model:

  1. This model is applicable only to hydrogen-like atoms and fails in case of higher atoms.
  2. It could not explain the fine structure of the spectral lines in the spectrum of hydrogen atom.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Excitation Energy: It is defined as the energy required by an electron of an atom to jump from its ground state to any one of its existed state. Ionisation Energy: It is defined as the energy required to remove an electron from an atom, i.e., the energy required to take an electron from its ground state to the outermost orbit (n = {tex}\infty{/tex} )
text_html:
Excitation Energy: It is defined as the energy required by an electron of an atom to jump from its ground state to any one of its existed state.
Ionisation Energy: It is defined as the energy required to remove an electron from an atom, i.e., the energy required to take an electron from its ground state to the outermost orbit (n = {tex}\infty{/tex})

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Excitation Potential: It is the accelerating potential which gives sufficient energy to a bombarding electron, so to excite the target atom by raising one of its electrons from an inner to and outer orbit. Ionisation Potential: It is the accelerating potential which gives to bombarding electron, sufficient energy to ionise the target atom by knocking one of its electrons completely out of the atom.
text_html:
Excitation Potential: It is the accelerating potential which gives sufficient energy to a bombarding electron, so to excite the target atom by raising one of its electrons from an inner to and outer orbit.
Ionisation Potential: It is the accelerating potential which gives to bombarding electron, sufficient energy to ionise the target atom by knocking one of its electrons completely out of the atom.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Rutherford's Atom Model: The atom consists of a central nucleus and revolving electron similar to a sun-planet system. The stability issues arise due to the interaction of charged particles using Coulomb's Law.
text_html: Rutherford's Atom Model: The atom consists of a central nucleus and revolving electron similar to a sun-planet system. The stability issues arise due to the interaction of charged particles using Coulomb's Law.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Gravitational vs. Coulomb Force: Planetary systems are held by gravity, whereas electrons and nuclei interact electromagnetically, leading to different stable conditions.
text_html: Gravitational vs. Coulomb Force: Planetary systems are held by gravity, whereas electrons and nuclei interact electromagnetically, leading to different stable conditions.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Acceleration and Radiation: A circular motion leads to centripetal acceleration, causing an accelerating charge to emit radiation, contradicting stable atom assumptions.
text_html: Acceleration and Radiation: A circular motion leads to centripetal acceleration, causing an accelerating charge to emit radiation, contradicting stable atom assumptions.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Emission Spectra and Stability: The classical electromagnetic theory predicts continuous spectra as electrons spiral towards the nucleus, differing from observed line spectra. This suggests incomplete explanations by the Rutherford model.
text_html: Emission Spectra and Stability: The classical electromagnetic theory predicts continuous spectra as electrons spiral towards the nucleus, differing from observed line spectra. This suggests incomplete explanations by the Rutherford model.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Bohr's Model Evolution: Niels Bohr adapted Rutherford’s model in 1913, introducing quantum concepts to address deficiencies in classical explanations at atomic scales.
text_html: Bohr's Model Evolution: Niels Bohr adapted Rutherford’s model in 1913, introducing quantum concepts to address deficiencies in classical explanations at atomic scales.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Bohr's Postulates: First Postulate: Electrons revolve in stable orbits without emitting radiant energy. Second Postulate: Orbits have quantised angular momentum. L = n(h/2π). Third Postulate: Transition of electrons between non-radiating orbits emits photons with energy equal to the difference in energy states.
text_html:
Bohr's Postulates:
  • First Postulate: Electrons revolve in stable orbits without emitting radiant energy.
  • Second Postulate: Orbits have quantised angular momentum. L = n(h/2π).
  • Third Postulate: Transition of electrons between non-radiating orbits emits photons with energy equal to the difference in energy states.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Hydrogen Atom Energies: Energies are quantised: E_n = -13.6/n² eV. Energy to free an electron from the ground state is 13.6 eV.
text_html: Hydrogen Atom Energies: Energies are quantised: E_n = -13.6/n² eV. Energy to free an electron from the ground state is 13.6 eV.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Stationary States: Hydrogen atoms usually exist in the ground state. Excited states require energy input and emit photons as electrons transition to lower levels.
text_html: Stationary States: Hydrogen atoms usually exist in the ground state. Excited states require energy input and emit photons as electrons transition to lower levels.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Quantum Transitions and Ionisation: Energy levels and transitions are calculated using principal quantum number n and involve quantised amounts of energy, aligning with experimental data.
text_html: Quantum Transitions and Ionisation: Energy levels and transitions are calculated using principal quantum number n and involve quantised amounts of energy, aligning with experimental data.

subject: Physics
topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
text: Energy Level Diagram: Each energy state of hydrogen corresponds to a specific principal quantum number, with states becoming closer as n increases. Ionisation occurs at n = ∞ with energy input equals to 0 eV.
text_html: Energy Level Diagram: Each energy state of hydrogen corresponds to a specific principal quantum number, with states becoming closer as n increases. Ionisation occurs at n = ∞ with energy input equals to 0 eV.

subject: Physics
topic: The Line Spectra of The Hydrogen Atom
text: Spectral Series of Hydrogen Atom: Whenever an electron in hydrogen atom makes a transition from a higher energy level n2 to a lower energy level n1, the difference of energy appears in the form of a photon of frequency v is given by- Different Spectral Series of Hydrogen Atom: These are as follows: Lyman Series. Here n2 = 2, 3, 4,... and n1 = 1. This series lies in the ultraviolet region. Balmer Series. Here n2 = 3, 4, 5, and n1 = 2. This series lies in the visible region. Paschen Series. Here n2 = 4, 5, 6, and n1 = 3. This series lies in the infrared region. Brackett Series. Here n2 = 5, 6, 7, and n1 = 4. This series lies in the infrared region. Pfund Series. Here n2 = 6, 7, 8 and n1 = 5. This series lies in the infrared region.
text_html:
Spectral Series of Hydrogen Atom: Whenever an electron in hydrogen atom makes a transition from a higher energy level n2 to a lower energy level n1, the difference of energy appears in the form of a photon of frequency v is given by-
Different Spectral Series of Hydrogen Atom:
These are as follows:

  1. Lyman Series. Here n2 = 2, 3, 4,... and n1 = 1. This series lies in the ultraviolet region.
  2. Balmer Series. Here n2 = 3, 4, 5, and n1 = 2. This series lies in the visible region.
  3. Paschen Series. Here n2 = 4, 5, 6, and n1 = 3. This series lies in the infrared region.
  4. Brackett Series. Here n2 = 5, 6, 7, and n1 = 4. This series lies in the infrared region.
  5. Pfund Series. Here n2 = 6, 7, 8 and n1 = 5. This series lies in the infrared region.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: De-Broglie Hypothesis- According to de Broglie, every moving particle is associated with a wave which controls the particle in every respect. The wave associated with a particle is called matter wave or de Broglie wave. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} . This is known as de-Broglie equation. de-Broglie wavelength of an electron of kinetic energy K- {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m e V)}}=\frac{1.23}{\sqrt{V}} \mathrm{~nm}=\frac{12.27}{\sqrt{V}} \mathrm{~A}^0{/tex}
text_html:
De-Broglie Hypothesis- According to de Broglie, every moving particle is associated with a wave which controls the particle in every respect. The wave associated with a particle is called matter wave or de Broglie wave. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}. This is known as de-Broglie equation. de-Broglie wavelength of an electron of kinetic energy K-
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m e V)}}=\frac{1.23}{\sqrt{V}} \mathrm{~nm}=\frac{12.27}{\sqrt{V}} \mathrm{~A}^0{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Distance of Closest Approach: When an alpha-particle of mass m and velocity v moves directly towards a nucleus of atomic number Z, its initial energy E, which is just the kinetic energy K gets completely converted into potential energy U at stopping point. This stopping point happens to be at a distance of closest approach d from the nucleus. Hence, d = {tex}\frac{2 Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 K}{/tex}
text_html:
Distance of Closest Approach: When an alpha-particle of mass m and velocity v moves directly towards a nucleus of atomic number Z, its initial energy E, which is just the kinetic energy K gets completely converted into potential energy U at stopping point. This stopping point happens to be at a distance of closest approach d from the nucleus.
Hence, d = {tex}\frac{2 Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 K}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Quantisation or Discretisation: The quantization or discretisation of a physical quantity means that it cannot have any arbitrary value but can change only to take certain specific value.
text_html: Quantisation or Discretisation: The quantization or discretisation of a physical quantity means that it cannot have any arbitrary value but can change only to take certain specific value.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantised Angular Momentum The angular momentum of an electron orbiting around a nucleus is quantised, defined by **L n =n h / 2π** where **n** is an integer.
text_html:
Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantised Angular Momentum
The angular momentum of an electron orbiting around a nucleus is quantised, defined by **Ln=n h / 2π** where **n** is an integer.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: de Broglie's Explanation In 1923, Louis de Broglie explained why electron angular momentum is quantised, proposing the wave nature of particles such as electrons.
text_html:
de Broglie's Explanation
In 1923, Louis de Broglie explained why electron angular momentum is quantised, proposing the wave nature of particles such as electrons.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Standing Particle Waves Electrons in their circular orbits form standing waves. Only specific wavelengths, which fit exactly around the orbit circumference, survive as standing waves.
text_html:
Standing Particle Waves
Electrons in their circular orbits form standing waves. Only specific wavelengths, which fit exactly around the orbit circumference, survive as standing waves.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: String Wave Analogy Standing waves form in a string when the wave's total travel distance down the string and back is one or more wavelengths. Similar principles apply to electron orbits.
text_html:
String Wave Analogy
Standing waves form in a string when the wave's total travel distance down the string and back is one or more wavelengths. Similar principles apply to electron orbits.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Conditions for Circular Orbits Electrons moving in nth circular orbit of radius **r n ** must satisfy **2πr n =nλ** where λ is the de Broglie wavelength.
text_html:
Conditions for Circular Orbits
Electrons moving in nth circular orbit of radius **rn** must satisfy **2πrn=nλ** where λ is the de Broglie wavelength.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Quantum Condition The quantum condition for an electron's angular momentum is **m v n r n =n h / 2π**, aligning with Bohr's postulate of angular momentum quantisation.
text_html:
Quantum Condition
The quantum condition for an electron's angular momentum is **m vn rn=n h / 2π**, aligning with Bohr's postulate of angular momentum quantisation.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Bohr's Model Limitations Bohr's model only applies to hydrogenic atoms and cannot explain complexities in multi-electron atoms like helium.
text_html:
Bohr's Model Limitations
Bohr's model only applies to hydrogenic atoms and cannot explain complexities in multi-electron atoms like helium.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Electron Interaction in Multi-electron Atoms Bohr's model excludes electrical force interactions between electrons in multi-electron atoms. This limitation prevents extension beyond hydrogenic atoms.
text_html:
Electron Interaction in Multi-electron Atoms
  • Bohr's model excludes electrical force interactions between electrons in multi-electron atoms.
  • This limitation prevents extension beyond hydrogenic atoms.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Intensity Variations in Spectra Bohr's model successfully predicts frequencies emitted by hydrogenic atoms but fails to explain why certain spectral lines have different intensities.
text_html:
Intensity Variations in Spectra
Bohr's model successfully predicts frequencies emitted by hydrogenic atoms but fails to explain why certain spectral lines have different intensities.

subject: Physics
topic: De Broglie's Explanation of Bohr's Second Postulate of Quantisation
text: Need for Quantum Mechanics Complex atoms require the application of Quantum Mechanics, beyond Bohr's model, providing a complete understanding of atomic structures and interactions.
text_html:
Need for Quantum Mechanics
Complex atoms require the application of Quantum Mechanics, beyond Bohr's model, providing a complete understanding of atomic structures and interactions.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Atomic Hypothesis Progress by 19th Century By the 19th century, significant evidence supported the atomic hypothesis of matter, suggesting that matter consists of discrete atoms.
text_html:
Atomic Hypothesis Progress by 19th Century
By the 19th century, significant evidence supported the atomic hypothesis of matter, suggesting that matter consists of discrete atoms.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: J. J. Thomson's Discovery of Electrons J. J. Thomson discovered that different atom types contain negatively charged electrons, identical across all atoms.
text_html:
J. J. Thomson's Discovery of Electrons
J. J. Thomson discovered that different atom types contain negatively charged electrons, identical across all atoms.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Electrical Neutrality of Atoms Atoms are neutral; hence they also possess positive charges to balance out the negatively charged electrons.
text_html:
Electrical Neutrality of Atoms
Atoms are neutral; hence they also possess positive charges to balance out the negatively charged electrons.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Thomson's Plum Pudding Model Proposed in 1898. Described atoms as a sphere of positive charge with electrons embedded like seeds in a watermelon.
text_html:
Thomson's Plum Pudding Model
  • Proposed in 1898.
  • Described atoms as a sphere of positive charge with electrons embedded like seeds in a watermelon.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Limitations of Thomson's Model The plum pudding model failed to accurately describe the distribution of electrons and positive charges in the atom.
text_html:
Limitations of Thomson's Model
The plum pudding model failed to accurately describe the distribution of electrons and positive charges in the atom.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Electromagnetic Radiation Emission Condensed matter and dense gases emit continuous spectra of electromagnetic radiation. Rarefied gases emit discrete spectra appearing as bright lines indicating atomic emissions.
text_html:
Electromagnetic Radiation Emission
  • Condensed matter and dense gases emit continuous spectra of electromagnetic radiation.
  • Rarefied gases emit discrete spectra appearing as bright lines indicating atomic emissions.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Characteristics of Atomic Spectra Each element is linked to a unique radiation spectrum. Example: Hydrogen, simplest element, provides precise spectral lines indicating its atomic structure.
text_html:
Characteristics of Atomic Spectra
  • Each element is linked to a unique radiation spectrum.
  • Example: Hydrogen, simplest element, provides precise spectral lines indicating its atomic structure.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Johann Jakob Balmer and Hydrogen Spectra In 1885, Balmer provided an empirical formula predicting hydrogen's atomic spectral lines.
text_html:
Johann Jakob Balmer and Hydrogen Spectra
In 1885, Balmer provided an empirical formula predicting hydrogen's atomic spectral lines.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Rutherford's Alpha Particle Scattering Experiment Proposed in 1906 and conducted by Geiger and Marsden in 1911. Investigated the atomic structure through alpha-particle scattering.
text_html:
Rutherford's Alpha Particle Scattering Experiment

  • Proposed in 1906 and conducted by Geiger and Marsden in 1911.
  • Investigated the atomic structure through alpha-particle scattering.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Emergence of Rutherford's Planetary Model Developed from alpha-particle scattering data. Suggested a nucleus containing the atom's positive charge and mass, while electrons orbit like planets.
text_html:
Emergence of Rutherford's Planetary Model

  • Developed from alpha-particle scattering data.
  • Suggested a nucleus containing the atom's positive charge and mass, while electrons orbit like planets.
